name: Koma (1 comma)
note: Smallest canonical comma unit in 53-comma descriptions.

name: Eksik Bakiyye (3 commas)
note: Interval class used in some Arel-Ezgi-Uzdilek analyses.

name: Bakiyye (4 commas)
note: Canonical small semitone class in Turkish theory.

name: Kucuk Mucennep (5 commas)
note: Small neutral-second class.

name: Buyuk Mucennep (8 commas)
note: Large neutral-second class.

name: Tanini (9 commas)
note: Canonical large tone class.

name: Artik Ikili (12 commas)
note: Augmented-second class (12-comma reading).

name: Artik Ikili (13 commas)
note: Augmented-second class (13-comma reading).

name: Quarter-tone Step
note: Pedagogical quarter-tone step used in many modern descriptions.

name: Minor Second (~95c)
note: One common small second value referenced in jins Saba discussions.

name: Semitone (~100c)
note: Equal-tempered semitone reference used comparatively in maqam pedagogy.

name: Saba Small Medium Second (~140c)
note: Contextual medium-second class appearing in jins Saba analyses.

name: Neutral Second (~150c)
note: Widely cited neutral-second class in maqam pedagogy.

name: Saba Large Medium Second (~160c)
note: Contextual medium-second class appearing in jins Saba analyses.

name: Whole Tone (~200c)
note: Large tone class used in many maqam constructions.

name: Hijaz Augmented Second (~300c)
note: Characteristic augmented-second span associated with Hijaz-family ajnas.

name: Neutral Third (~350c)
note: Widely cited neutral-third class in maqam pedagogy.

name: Koron Adjustment (~40c)
note: Koron lowers a note by roughly a quarter-step in modern notation practice.

name: Sori Adjustment (~40c)
note: Sori raises a note by roughly a quarter-step in modern notation practice.

name: Persian Neutral Second (~150c)
note: Common neutral-second class in dastgah pedagogy.

name: Persian Neutral Third (~350c)
note: Common neutral-third class in dastgah pedagogy.

name: Leimma (8 moria)
note: Small tone class in 72-moria descriptions.

name: Elasson Tonos (10 moria)
note: Minor tone class in 72-moria descriptions.

name: Meizon Tonos (12 moria)
note: Major tone class in 72-moria descriptions.

name: Slendro Step (approx.)
tradition: Indonesian gamelan tuning practice (slendro/pelog, region-specific embat)
note: Slendro is often described as five roughly equal steps per octave; exact tuning varies by ensemble.

name: Slendro Two-Step Span (approx.)
tradition: Indonesian gamelan tuning practice (slendro/pelog, region-specific embat)
note: Two-step span in slendro-derived analyses; highly ensemble-dependent.

name: Slendro Three-Step Span (approx.)
tradition: Indonesian gamelan tuning practice (slendro/pelog, region-specific embat)
note: Three-step span in slendro-derived analyses; highly ensemble-dependent.

name: Pelog Small Step (approx.)
tradition: Indonesian gamelan tuning practice (slendro/pelog, region-specific embat)
note: Pelog contains uneven steps; this captures a common small-step class used in pedagogy.

name: Pelog Medium Step (approx.)
tradition: Indonesian gamelan tuning practice (slendro/pelog, region-specific embat)
note: Pelog medium-step class used in comparative analyses.

name: Pelog Large Step (approx.)
tradition: Indonesian gamelan tuning practice (slendro/pelog, region-specific embat)
note: Pelog large-step class used in comparative analyses.

name: Kempyung (approx. fifth)
tradition: Indonesian gamelan tuning practice (slendro/pelog, region-specific embat)
note: Kempyung is commonly treated as a fifth-like consonant relation in gamelan discourse.

name: Gembyang (octave)
tradition: Indonesian gamelan tuning practice (slendro/pelog, region-specific embat)
note: Gembyang denotes octave equivalence in gamelan practice.

name: Thai Seven-Tone Step 1
note: Derived from the common pedagogical model of seven near-equal steps per octave; actual practice varies.

name: Thai Seven-Tone Step 2
note: Derived from the common pedagogical model of seven near-equal steps per octave; actual practice varies.

name: Thai Seven-Tone Step 3
note: Derived from the common pedagogical model of seven near-equal steps per octave; actual practice varies.

note: Derived from the common pedagogical model of seven near-equal steps per octave; actual practice varies.

name: Thai Seven-Tone Step 5
note: Derived from the common pedagogical model of seven near-equal steps per octave; actual practice varies.

name: Thai Seven-Tone Step 6
note: Derived from the common pedagogical model of seven near-equal steps per octave; actual practice varies.

name: Huangzhong
tradition: Chinese 12 lü pitch-pipe system (sanfen sunyi / cycle-of-fifths lineage)
note: Reference lü in many historical lists.
